prompt:
Pin classic-laddie's kernel back to stable so ZFS actually builds. Replaces PR #531's eval-time-only workaround.

## What's broken (real evidence)
PR #531 added `nixpkgs.config.problems.handlers.zfs.broken = \"warn\"` to silence the ZFS broken check. CI passed because `nix build --dry-run` evaluates without building kernel modules. But on a real local rebuild today, the build fails:

```
configure: error:
   *** Cannot build against kernel version 7.0.3-zen1.
   *** The maximum supported kernel version is 6.19.
```

So the `broken` flag was *correct* — ZFS literally cannot compile against zen 7.0.3. The eval-time silence trick lets eval pass; the build fails for real. classic-laddie boots from a ZFS root, so the kernel must have a working ZFS module.

## Fix
Pin classic-laddie's kernel to stable 6.18 (or whatever stable LTS is current). `pkgs.linuxPackages` is the default stable. This loses zen-specific patches but keeps sched-ext (upstreamed in 6.12, available on stable since), so `services.scx` continues to work.

Edit `hosts/classic-laddie/hardware.nix`:

1. **Remove** the `nixpkgs.config.problems.handlers.zfs.broken = \"warn\"` line and its preceding comment block. It silenced the eval but didn't fix the underlying issue.

2. **Add** a kernel pin that overrides whatever `gaming.nix` (or wherever) sets as the default. Use `lib.mkOverride 60` so it wins against gaming.nix's default-priority assignment but loses to the `mkForce` inside the `stable-kernel` specialisation:

```nix
# ZFS upstream's kernelMaxSupportedMajorMinor = \"6.19\"; the linux-zen
# kernel pulled in by recent flake.lock updates (7.0.3 as of #528) is past
# that, so ZFS 2.4.1 won't compile (configure-time \"Cannot build against
# kernel version 7.0.3-zen1\"). Pin to stable until ZFS upstream releases
# a release with a higher kernel cap. PR #531 tried setting the broken
# flag to \"warn\" instead — that silenced eval but didn't fix the actual
# build, since CI dry-run doesn't compile kmods.
#
# sched-ext is upstream in 6.12+, so services.scx still works on stable.
#
# mkOverride 60 wins against the default-priority assignment in
# modules/common/gaming.nix while still losing to the mkForce inside
# the stable-kernel specialisation, preserving that fallback.
boot.kernelPackages = lib.mkOverride 60 pkgs.linuxPackages;
```

(If `pkgs.linuxPackages_6_18` is the canonical stable attribute in current nixpkgs, use that instead of the bare `pkgs.linuxPackages` — be specific. Read `flake.lock` and check `nixpkgs` to see what's available.)

## Update issue #532
After landing this PR, also leave a comment on issue #532 (the tracking issue for the broken=warn cleanup) noting that the override was *insufficient* — the actual fix was the kernel pin. The cleanup the issue tracked was always wrong-shaped because there was no working state for the override to clean up. Either close #532 or repurpose it to track 'remove kernel pin once nixpkgs ZFS supports zen 7.x.'

## CRITICAL: no local nix tooling
The OS-level memory protection from PR #529 hasn't kicked in on the running system (the user got partway through a rebuild and it failed before activation). So the wedge-resistance is still not in effect. CI does dry-run validation on a runner — that's the gate.

But note: **CI dry-run will not catch the kmod build failure** (it didn't catch this one either). The validation that this fix works has to be the user successfully rebuilding on classic-laddie. We can't avoid that. Don't try to verify locally yourself.

## PR description requirements
- Quote the actual build failure ('Cannot build against kernel version 7.0.3-zen1')
- Explain why CI dry-run missed it (no kmod builds in dry-run)
- Note that this replaces #531's eval-time-only workaround
- Reference the kernel pin's `mkOverride 60` priority math relative to `gaming.nix` and the stable-kernel specialisation
- Note the sched-ext availability on stable kernels (services.scx unaffected)

## Acceptance
- `hosts/classic-laddie/hardware.nix` has the kernel pin and removes the broken=warn override
- CI green (eval-time validation, knowing this won't catch the kmod issue)
- Comment on issue #532 explaining the actual fix
- Commit message and PR description are clear about why eval-time silencing was the wrong approach
